preservation_requirements: Disciplinary expertise becomes more important with AI, not less
Expertise is judgement under uncertainty, not a body of facts — ability to recognise anomalies, make decisions with incomplete information, know what matters vs what can be ignored
AI knowledge is statistical: it identifies what is typical, not what is important; importance depends on knowing open questions, dead ends, and current tensions
Force multiplier concept: expert + AI outperforms either alone because expert can verify AI output
Validateability axis from companion post depends on genuine expertise
Centaur model: human-AI teams outperform both alone (strategic judgement + tactical calculation)
Expertise erosion paradox: AI substitutes for foundational intellectual activities through which expertise develops
Lin and Sohail (Patterns 2026): GenAI performs foundational intellectual activities through which scientific expertise develops — unlike previous tools that augmented specific capabilities
Cotton and Scholle-Cotton (Issues in Science and Technology 2026): early-career professionals whose output benefits most from AI may be least prepared to lead; 'short-circuiting the apprenticeship'
Skill atrophy analogies: language fluency fades without practice; cycling baseline persists but high-performance skills erode — same applies to scientific analytical judgement
Unwritten/tacit knowledge: learned through practice and observation, never captured in training corpora — includes lab technique, pattern recognition, professional judgement
2025 preprint on AI lab agents acknowledged gap between documented knowledge and implicit lab practice
Unwritten knowledge could eventually be digitised but the individual still needs embodied practice to develop reflexes
Unwritten knowledge feeds higher-order judgement: experimental design, evaluating others' work, recognising surprising results

Strong vs weak task bundles from labour economics: experienced scientist's work is a strong bundle (interdependent tasks), graduate student's is weaker; hollowing out junior bundle breaks the pipeline producing strong-bundle experts
Krishnan (2026): PhD training should establish foundational mastery benchmarks before AI tools introduced
2026 study: fluent AI users experience more failures than novices but failures are visible and recoverable; expertise lets you know when AI has failed
Nature 2025 study of 41.3 million papers: AI users published 67% more, 3x more citations, but AI contracted topic range in 70% of sub-fields; follow-on engagement dropped 24%; star-like structure
Steering problem: productivity decoupled from judgement about what is worth investigating leads to local maxima
Complacency cascade (Lin and Sohail): users gradually accept AI outputs with less critical evaluation; population-level systemic risk

Distorted evaluation signals: if metrics reward output and AI inflates output without corresponding expertise growth, hiring/promotion signals are systematically distorted; Cotton and Scholle-Cotton warn of leadership gaps

Five training recommendations: (1) more rigorous training not less, (2) deliberate sequencing — apprenticeship before delegation, (3) assessment shift toward observable reasoning, (4) protect time for irreducibly human activities, (5) resist the narrowing via funding and PI decisions
Autonomous AI research systems already exist (hypothesis generation through writeup with minimal human involvement)
Disease X trade-offs: choosing which disease, for whom, with what priority involves ethical and population trade-offs AI cannot resolve
Mountain-climbing analogy: if AI can produce same outputs, argument for expertise shifts from practical to existential — value of the practice itself
Reference to future post on longer-term questions about autonomous AI research
Reference to companion post on when to use AI (ai_decision_flow)
All MARGIN comments preserved
Core closing argument: expertise is not what AI is replacing; expertise is what makes AI worth using

steps.original_content:
# Disciplinary Expertise in an AI-Assisted World

*Why deep training matters more now, not less*

---

One of the questions I hear most often — from students, colleagues, and audiences after talks — is some version of this: "If AI already knows my field, why do I need to?" It takes different forms. A graduate student wonders whether investing years in mastering statistical methods still makes sense when AI can generate the code in seconds. A postdoc asks whether learning to read mass spectra by eye is a useful skill or an anachronism. A department chair, planning curriculum revisions, wonders how much disciplinary content can be trimmed now that students can look anything up.

These are honest questions, and they deserve honest answers. This post is my attempt at one.

The short version: disciplinary expertise becomes more important with AI, not less. But saying that is not enough — the claim needs to be unpacked, because AI's capabilities are real and growing, and a dismissive "you still need to know your field" does not actually explain *what* expertise does that AI cannot, or *why* the gap matters. Those are the questions I want to work through here.

A note on scope: this post focuses on the present and the near-term future — the world in which scientists are actively using AI alongside their own expertise, and the tensions that creates. There are longer-term questions about what happens when AI systems can autonomously design and run entire research programmes with minimal human involvement. I touch on those at the end, but a full discussion will be the subject of a future post.

This is a companion to my earlier post on [when to use AI in the natural sciences](../ai_decision_flow/), which develops a decision framework for evaluating specific tasks. That framework depends on a concept it names but does not fully explore: the idea that disciplinary expertise is a "force multiplier" for AI use. This post is the fuller exploration.

<!-- MARGIN: Force multiplier — a term borrowed from military strategy, used here to mean something that amplifies the effectiveness of whatever it is combined with. Disciplinary expertise multiplied by AI produces more than either alone. AI without expertise produces output that no one can properly evaluate. -->

## What expertise actually is

To understand why expertise still matters, it helps to be precise about what it is. Expertise is not a body of facts. It is the ability to make judgements under uncertainty, recognise what is surprising or anomalous in data, and know which details matter and which can be safely ignored. An experienced organic chemist does not just know reaction mechanisms — she recognises when a yield is unexpectedly low and can generate plausible explanations. An ecologist with twenty years of fieldwork does not just know species names — he notices when a community composition suggests something has changed about the site.

<!-- MARGIN: Judgement under uncertainty — the ability to make informed decisions when the available information is incomplete, ambiguous, or contradictory. This is distinct from following a procedure or applying a rule. Most consequential scientific decisions — what to investigate, what to trust, what to publish — involve uncertainty. -->

AI has a form of knowledge, and it would be dishonest to dismiss it. Frontier models have been trained on vast bodies of scientific literature, and they can produce competent-looking summaries, generate reasonable code, and identify patterns across large datasets. In experimental tests, AI systems have demonstrated the ability to answer domain-specific questions, draft methods sections, and even propose experimental designs.

But AI's knowledge is statistical. It can tell you what is typical in a body of literature — the common approaches, the standard methods, the established conclusions. What it struggles with is telling you what is *important*: which of those common approaches has a known limitation that matters for your specific question, which established conclusion has been quietly challenged by recent work that has not yet propagated through the field, which unexpected observation in your data is an artefact of your instrument and which might be the most interesting result in the dataset.

That distinction — between typical and important — is what expertise provides. And it is not a distinction that more training data resolves, because importance is not a property of the text. It is a judgement that depends on knowing the field's open questions, its history of dead ends, and its current tensions.

steps.section_name: Part one intro and The force multiplier
steps.original_content:
## Part one: what happens to the individual

The relationship between AI and individual expertise has two sides, and they pull in opposite directions.

On one side, AI amplifies what an expert can do. A scientist with deep domain knowledge who uses AI for tasks she can verify — reformatting, first-pass literature organisation, routine code generation, data wrangling — becomes genuinely more productive. She reclaims time that can go toward the parts of the work where her expertise matters most: designing experiments, interpreting results, mentoring students. This is the force multiplier effect, and it is real.

On the other side, AI can erode or prevent the development of the very expertise it needs in order to be a force multiplier. This is what several researchers have begun calling the "expertise erosion paradox," and it deserves careful attention.

### The force multiplier

When a well-trained scientist delegates a task to AI, the delegation works because she can verify the output. She knows what correct looks like. If the AI-generated code runs but specifies the wrong model, she catches it. If the literature summary omits a key paper, she notices. If the suggested analysis is technically valid but inappropriate for the research question, she recognises the mismatch.

In the [companion post on when to use AI](../ai_decision_flow/), I call this the "validateability" axis — the question of whether you can efficiently verify what the AI produces. The axis only works if the person in the loop has genuine expertise. When they do, AI is a straightforward productivity gain: it handles the mechanical execution, the expert provides the judgement, and the combination outperforms either alone.

This is what some researchers call the centaur model — a human-AI team, named after the mythological hybrid. The term gained traction after MIT's 2026 workshop on AI in the physical sciences, but the idea is older: in centaur chess, human-AI pairs consistently outperformed both humans and AI playing alone, because the human provided strategic judgement while the computer provided tactical calculation. The analogy extends naturally to research. AI provides computational and analytical reach; the researcher provides the direction and the judgement about what the results mean.

<!-- MARGIN: Centaur scientist — the term comes from centaur chess, where human-AI teams outperformed both humans and AI alone. MIT's 2026 workshop on AI in the mathematical and physical sciences applied the concept to research, arguing that the most effective model pairs deep disciplinary judgement with AI's analytical reach. -->

### The erosion paradox

But here is the problem. The tasks that AI handles well — reading and summarising literature, writing first drafts, generating and debugging code, designing routine analyses — are not just mechanical chores. They are also the activities through which scientific expertise is *built*. A student who wrestles with a statistical model, gets it wrong, debugs it, and finally understands what the parameters mean has learned something that a student who asks AI for the code and runs it without error has not.

Lin and Sohail, writing in *Patterns* in 2026, articulate this clearly: "Unlike previous tools that augmented specific capabilities, GenAI can perform foundational intellectual activities through which scientific expertise itself develops: reading literature, designing studies, writing arguments, and generating code." Previous tools — microscopes, statistical software, sequencing machines — extended what a scientist could *do*. GenAI can substitute for what a scientist needs to *learn*.

Cotton and Scholle-Cotton, writing in *Issues in Science and Technology* in 2026, put the implication sharply: "The early-career professionals whose output benefits most from AI today may be the least prepared to lead their fields in an AI-driven future." They call this *short-circuiting the apprenticeship* — and the analogy is apt, because apprenticeship is built on productive struggle, on wrestling with material that resists you, on building understanding through effort rather than receiving answers from a tool.

<!-- MARGIN: Short-circuiting the apprenticeship — a phrase from Cotton & Scholle-Cotton (2026) describing how AI allows trainees to skip the difficult, time-consuming tasks that historically built expertise. The output is polished, but the person has not developed the judgement that would allow them to produce or evaluate such output independently. -->

This should not be surprising. We all have direct experience with skill atrophy. A person who learned a second language as a teenager but did not speak it for a decade finds that the fluency has faded — not the basic vocabulary, perhaps, but the speed, the idiom, the ability to catch a subtle implication. A musician who trained seriously but then stopped playing for years can still pick up the instrument, but the precision and expressiveness that came from daily practice are gone. Even something as physical as cycling: a person who trained as a teenager, developed real bike-handling skill — jumps, tight cornering, reading terrain — and then did not ride for a decade can still ride a bike. The baseline ability persists. But the high-performance skills, the ones that required sustained practice to build, have eroded.

The same logic applies to scientific skills. The concern is not that a researcher who delegates data analysis to AI for several years will forget what a p-value is. It is that the honed, high-performance version of analytical judgement — the ability to look at a result and immediately sense that something is off, to notice the pattern in the residuals, to catch the subtle specification error — requires regular practice to maintain. And it is precisely those high-performance skills that make the centaur model work. A force multiplier built on eroded expertise is not much of a multiplier.

### The unwritten dimension

The erosion problem runs deeper than the skills that can be taught in a textbook or a lecture. A large fraction of what makes a scientist expert is *unwritten knowledge* — the kind that has never been captured in any manual, protocol, or training corpus. How to read a chromatography peak. When to trust a measurement and when to suspect contamination. How to recognise that a reaction flask looks wrong before you can say exactly why. In the academic literature, this is sometimes called *tacit knowledge*, and it is learned not through instruction but through practice, observation, and years of experience.

<!-- MARGIN: Unwritten (tacit) knowledge — knowledge that is difficult to articulate in writing or speech, learned through practice and observation rather than instruction. In scientific contexts, this includes laboratory technique, pattern recognition in data, and professional judgement about how to interpret ambiguous results. It represents a large fraction of what makes an expert an expert. -->

This kind of knowledge is transmitted through the mentoring relationship — years of working alongside someone more experienced, watching how they react to unexpected results, absorbing their standards for what counts as "good enough" evidence. AI cannot currently access it because it was never captured in any training corpus. A 2025 preprint on AI laboratory agents acknowledged this directly: "Current AI tools have proven effective in literature analysis and code generation, but do not address the critical gap between documented knowledge and implicit lab practice."

<!-- MARGIN: Digitising unwritten knowledge — in principle, this knowledge could be made explicit and captured: scientists could document their reasoning as they work, or future technologies (wearable cameras, narrated lab workflows, video archives of bench technique) could record it passively. If digitised, AI could learn from it. This is a plausible future, and it means unwritten knowledge is not a permanent boundary for AI — but it does not resolve the question of whether *the scientist* needs to have built that knowledge through practice. -->

It is plausible that unwritten knowledge will eventually be digitised — documented, recorded, made available to AI systems. But even in that future, the erosion concern remains. Unwritten knowledge matters to the individual scientist not as information to be looked up but as reflexes built through practice. The chemist who notices a colour change while her conscious attention is elsewhere is not retrieving a fact. She is operating on thousands of hours of wired-in pattern recognition. That automatic quality — the ability to perceive and respond without deliberate effort — comes from doing the work, not from accessing a record of someone else doing it.

And those reflexes are not confined to the bench. The intuitions built through hands-on practice shape how a scientist thinks about problems even when she is far from the laboratory. The PI who designs a strong experiment does so partly because years of running experiments — including failed ones — have given her an instinct for what will work, what controls are needed, what variables are likely to matter. The reviewer who spots a flaw in a methods section draws on the same embodied sense of how an experiment *should* feel. Unwritten knowledge, in other words, is not just useful for doing laboratory work. It is the substrate on which higher-order scientific judgement is built — the foundation for experimental design, for evaluating others' work, for recognising which results are surprising and which are expected.

This makes it a particularly vulnerable casualty of the erosion paradox. If trainees delegate the bench work, the first-pass analyses, and the hands-on troubleshooting, they may never develop the embodied fluency that feeds into every other part of their scientific thinking. The explicit skills — how to run a particular statistical test, how to format a citation — can be re-learned from a textbook if lost. The unwritten dimension cannot. It is the harder loss, and the harder one to recover from.

### The two sides together

This is the core tension at the individual level: AI both requires and erodes expertise. It amplifies what you know, but it can prevent you from developing that knowledge in the first place — or degrade knowledge you already had. The implication is that AI use cannot be all-or-nothing. It has to be deliberate: some tasks delegated, others done by hand specifically because the doing is what builds the capacity to judge.

There is a useful way to think about this from labour economics. A job is not a single task — it is a *bundle* of tasks. Some bundles are weak: the components can be cheaply separated, and the separable parts are the ones AI takes over. Other bundles are strong: the tasks are so interdependent that pulling one out degrades the rest. An experienced scientist's work is a strong bundle — the experimental design, the data interpretation, the mentoring, the judgement about what to pursue next all feed into each other, and separating any one of them diminishes the others. But a graduate student's work, early in training, is a weaker bundle. Much of it — literature summaries, routine data processing, first-pass code — *can* be separated and handed to AI. The uncomfortable implication is that the career stage most vulnerable to AI disruption is also the one where you are building the interdependencies that make you indispensable later. If AI hollows out the junior bundle too aggressively, the pipeline that produces people with strong bundles breaks.

Krishnan, writing in 2026, argues that PhD training should establish foundational mastery benchmarks *before* AI tools are introduced for core tasks. The logic is straightforward: you cannot validate what you have not first learned to do yourself. A student who has never manually searched and read a body of literature cannot meaningfully judge whether AI's literature summary missed something important. The apprenticeship has to come before the delegation.

<!-- MARGIN: Deliberate sequencing — the practice of structuring training so that foundational skills are built through direct practice before AI tools are used for those same tasks. The aim is not to reject AI but to ensure that students develop the expertise needed to use it responsibly. -->

A related finding offers a useful framing for how this balance plays out in practice. A 2026 study found that *fluent* AI users — those with deep expertise and comfort with the tools — actually experience more failures than novices. But their failures are visible, recoverable, and occur alongside greater success on complex tasks. The interpretation: expertise lets you know when AI has failed, which may be the single most important meta-competency in AI-assisted work. Novices do not fail less; they fail without noticing.

steps.section_name: Part two: Population-level effects (steering, complacency, distorted signals)
steps.original_content:
## Part two: what happens across a field

The individual-level picture — AI amplifies expertise but can erode it — is important, but it is not the whole story. When many researchers adopt AI simultaneously, patterns emerge at the population level that no single individual intended or controls. These are emergent effects, and several have now been documented.

### The steering problem

A large-scale study published in *Nature* in 2025, analysing 41.3 million research papers, found a striking pattern: AI-using scientists published 67% more papers and received roughly three times more citations than non-users. At the individual level, this looks like a clear win. But across 70% of sub-fields studied, AI collectively *contracted* the range of topics being investigated. Papers clustered around computationally tractable, data-rich problems. Follow-on engagement between papers dropped by 24%.

In other words, AI was making individual scientists more productive while making science as a whole less diverse. The researchers describe a "star-like structure" — many papers orbiting the same hot topics, high output but low engagement between distinct lines of work.

This is what happens when productivity is decoupled from the judgement that decides what is worth being productive about. AI makes certain kinds of work so much easier that the path of least resistance shifts — not just for individuals, but for entire fields. Without enough scientists who can recognise what *matters* as distinct from what is data-rich and statistically tractable, research drifts toward local maxima: problems that are easy to work on rather than problems that are important to solve.

<!-- MARGIN: Local maxima — a term from optimisation theory, used here metaphorically. A local maximum is a solution that looks optimal in its immediate neighbourhood but is not the best solution overall. In the research context, it describes a problem that is easy to make progress on (because it is data-rich and computationally tractable) but that may not be the most important question in the field. Expertise is what allows a scientist to look beyond the local maximum and identify where the genuinely important questions lie. -->

The steering problem is not a deskilling problem — it can happen even among experts. But it is amplified when the deskilling dynamic reduces the number of researchers with the deep judgement needed to resist the pull of tractable problems. The two levels interact: individual erosion of expertise feeds the collective narrowing of science.

### The complacency cascade

Lin and Sohail document a pattern they call the "complacency cascade": users initially scrutinise AI outputs carefully, then gradually accept them with less critical evaluation, particularly for technical content where detecting subtle errors requires concentrated attention. The trajectory is not ignorance but drift — a slow erosion of habits that were once automatic.

At the individual level, complacency is a personal risk. At the population level, it becomes a systemic one. If most researchers in a field gradually stop scrutinising AI output with full attention, the collective quality of scientific work degrades — not through any single dramatic failure, but through a thousand small acceptances of output that was almost right.

### Distorted signals

The emergent effects extend to how scientists are evaluated. If career advancement metrics reward publication output, and if AI inflates that output without corresponding growth in expertise, then hiring committees and promotion panels are evaluating a signal that has been systematically distorted. Cotton and Scholle-Cotton warn that this could create leadership gaps in knowledge fields — researchers who rose quickly on AI-assisted productivity but lack the deep understanding to guide a research programme, mentor students through genuine difficulty, or recognise when a promising-looking line of work is headed for a dead end.

This is an institutional problem, not an individual one. Even a scientist who personally manages the balance between AI use and skill development is operating in a system where the signals used to evaluate everyone have been altered. The metrics that once served as rough proxies for expertise — publications, citations, grant productivity — become less reliable as AI decouples output from understanding.

steps.section_name: What this means for how we train scientists
steps.original_content:
## What this means for how we train scientists

If the picture I have drawn is correct — AI amplifies expertise at the individual level but can erode it through the same mechanism, produces emergent effects at the population level that no single researcher controls, and the unwritten dimension of expertise remains the hardest to build and the easiest to lose — then how we develop expertise deserves serious attention. A few implications follow.

**The case for more rigorous disciplinary training, not less.** It is tempting to conclude that if AI handles routine tasks, training can be shortened or made more superficial. The opposite is true. The routine tasks were often the vehicle through which deeper understanding was built. A student who hand-codes a statistical analysis, gets it wrong, debugs it, and finally understands what the model is doing has learned something that a student who asks AI for the code and runs it without error has not. Not every task needs to be done the hard way — but some do, and knowing which ones requires, again, the expertise of the mentor.

**Deliberate sequencing matters.** The apprenticeship has to come before the delegation. Foundational mastery benchmarks should be established before AI tools are introduced for core tasks. You cannot validate what you have not first learned to do yourself.

**Assessment needs to shift toward observable reasoning.** If AI can produce polished written outputs that are indistinguishable from expert work, then a polished output is no longer strong evidence of understanding. Assessment may need to move toward formats where reasoning is visible: oral defences, live problem-solving exercises, structured decision reviews where students explain not just what they concluded but how they got there and what alternatives they considered.

**Protect time for what is — for now — irreducibly human.** If AI handles more of the mechanical work, the time reclaimed should go toward the activities where expertise is currently built and exercised at its highest level: mentoring conversations, collaborative thinking, sitting with a puzzling result, reading deeply rather than broadly. These activities are not luxuries that can be scheduled around the "real work." In an AI-assisted world, they *are* the real work — and even if AI eventually encroaches on some of them, building the human capacity to do them well remains the foundation for using AI responsibly in the meantime.

**Resist the narrowing.** The population-level finding — that AI steers science toward tractable problems — is a collective action problem, but individuals and institutions can push against it. Funding bodies can specifically support data-sparse, anomaly-rich, and foundationally oriented research that AI tends to deprioritise. PIs can protect space in their research programmes for questions chosen because they are important, not because they are easy to work on with current tools.

## The long-term role of the human scientist

If AI continues to improve — absorbing more domain knowledge, digitising more of the unwritten expertise, becoming better at tasks that currently require human judgement — what is the scientist's enduring role?

For the near term, I think the answer is clearer than it might first appear, because it follows directly from the picture this post has drawn. AI's capabilities, however they grow, will continue to require someone who can decide what questions are worth asking, recognise when an answer is wrong for reasons that are not in the data, and take responsibility for the consequences of acting on a result. These are not tasks that AI performs poorly at today and will perform well at tomorrow. They are tasks whose nature requires a person — someone with stakes, with a position in a community, with the standing to say "I vouch for this work."

The scientist's role, in other words, is converging on what it has always been at its best: not the person who runs every analysis and formats every reference, but the person who *decides what matters*. Who chooses what to investigate, who judges what to trust, who takes the finding and places it in a context that makes it meaningful. AI extends the reach of each of those acts. It does not perform them.

But I want to be honest about a harder version of the question. Autonomous AI research systems already exist — systems that can generate hypotheses, design experiments, run them in programmable laboratories, analyse the results, and write up the findings with minimal human involvement. These are not speculative; early versions are operating now. If the trajectory continues, one could imagine a future in which the directive given to an AI system is something as broad as "find a treatment for this disease" — and the system orchestrates the entire research programme, from literature review to clinical trial design, with human workers available as resources rather than directors.

In that scenario, what role is left for disciplinary expertise? The directive itself still requires human judgement — choosing which disease, for whom, with what priority relative to other problems. Those are not scientifically neutral decisions. They involve trade-offs between populations, between short-term and long-term benefit, between what is technically feasible and what is ethically acceptable. A system that discovers a promising treatment but requires testing protocols that violate ethical norms, or that optimises for one population at the expense of another, has not solved the problem — it has produced a result that still requires human judgement to evaluate and act on.

But I recognise that this response has limits. If AI systems become capable enough, the human role in directing research could narrow to something very thin — a broad goal-setting function that requires less and less domain knowledge over time. At that point, the argument for disciplinary expertise shifts from a practical one (you need it to do good science) to something closer to an existential one: that the practice of science — the struggle, the surprise, the slow accumulation of understanding — is valuable to the person doing it, regardless of whether AI could produce the same outputs more efficiently. That is a different kind of argument, closer to the question of why people climb mountains when helicopters exist.

I think that longer-term question deserves serious treatment, and I plan to return to it in a [future post](). For now, the practical case is strong enough on its own.

The risk is not that AI will make scientists obsolete. It is that we will stop training people to do the things that make them scientists — the judgement, the embodied understanding, the hard-won instinct for what is real and what is artefact — because AI makes it possible to produce scientific-looking output without any of that underneath.

Expertise is not what AI is replacing. Expertise is what makes AI worth using. The question is whether we continue to build it.

concision_directives: Remove the musician/instrument analogy in the skill atrophy passage — structural guidance says keep language and cycling, cut one, and instrument is the weakest
Shorten centaur chess explanation: cut 'The term gained traction after MIT's 2026 workshop... tactical calculation' origin story to one sentence; the MARGIN note already carries the definition

Remove the duplicate of 'you cannot validate what you have not first learned to do yourself' in training recommendation #2 (deliberate sequencing) — it already appears verbatim in the erosion paradox subsection and is restated a third time in recommendation #1

Merge the 'complacency cascade' and 'distorted signals' subsections into a single subsection (e.g., 'Population-level effects') — both describe emergent consequences and can share a single framing paragraph

Condense each of the five training recommendations to 1–2 sentences: cut the explanatory follow-on sentences that restate logic already established earlier in the post (e.g., recommendation #1 re-explains that routine tasks build understanding, which the erosion paradox section already covered in full)
Tighten the opening paragraph: the three-example list (grad student, postdoc, department chair) can be collapsed into one sentence naming all three without separate scenarios for each
Remove the sentence 'But saying that is not enough — the claim needs to be unpacked...' in the intro — the reader already knows the post will unpack it because the post exists

Condense the 'What expertise actually is' section: the two-paragraph structure (expertise is not facts / AI has statistical knowledge) can each lose 1–2 sentences — the chemist and ecologist examples make the point; the follow-on generalisation restates it

In the unwritten dimension section, tighten the two paragraphs about reflexes and intuition (starting 'It is plausible that unwritten knowledge will eventually be digitised' through 'the foundation for experimental design') into one paragraph — both say the same thing (embodied practice feeds higher-order judgement)

Cut the sentence 'This is what some researchers call the centaur model — a human-AI team, named after the mythological hybrid' — the MARGIN note already defines centaur scientist, making this line redundant
Tighten the Lin and Sohail quote setup: replace the full attribution sentence ('Lin and Sohail, writing in Patterns in 2026, articulate this clearly:') with a parenthetical citation after the quote
Tighten the Cotton and Scholle-Cotton quote setup similarly — paraphrase the framing sentence and keep the key phrases 'short-circuiting the apprenticeship' and the early-career professionals quote

In the long-term role section, compress the two paragraphs about the near-term answer ('For the near term, I think the answer is clearer...' through '...It does not perform them') into one tighter paragraph — the second paragraph mostly restates the first
Remove 'These are honest questions, and they deserve honest answers. This post is my attempt at one.' — the post speaks for itself
[Human directive] The centaur chess explanation can be shortened — the concept is clear, the origin story does not need as much space

[Human directive] Lin and Sohail and Cotton and Scholle-Cotton quotes can be paraphrased more tightly rather than set up with full attribution sentences each time — but preserve the key phrases (expertise erosion paradox, short-circuiting the apprenticeship)

[Human directive] The skill atrophy analogies (language, instrument, cycling) — all three make the same point. Keep two, cut one. The language and cycling analogies are strongest (language for universality, cycling for the baseline vs high-performance distinction)
[Human directive] The complacency cascade section and distorted signals section can be merged into a single tighter subsection on population-level effects
[Human directive] The five training recommendations are verbose — tighten each to 1-2 sentences while preserving all five points

[Human directive] The long-term role section (autonomous labs, solve disease X, mountain-climbing analogy) should be kept but compressed — preserve the reference to a future post and the key examples (disease X trade-offs, mountain-climbing analogy) but tighten the surrounding prose
[Human directive] The strong/weak bundle paragraph in The two sides together should be preserved — it is a recent addition and well-integrated
[Human directive] Preserve all MARGIN comments — they are part of the post format
[Human directive] Preserve all cross-reference links to companion posts
